summary: I was born in Ibaraki Prefecture, which is located in the center of Japan within the Kanto plain. It was in 1886, during the 19th year of the Meiji era. My early life there was peaceful, and I had little experience of suffering as a child under the protection of my loving mother. Ibaraki was where my roots and family traditions, including our Buddhist faith, were established.

I have a faint memory of the Sino-Japanese War from my childhood, but I was too young to remember much detail. It was one of the early wars I heard about growing up in Japan. Though I don't recall specific events, it was part of the backdrop of my early years before I moved on to more vivid memories of later conflicts.

The Russo-Japanese War happened when I was a young man living in Tokyo and attending middle school. I remember it quite clearly because I was old enough to understand the significance of the conflict. It was a major event during my youth and left a strong impression on me as I was growing up in Japan.

During World War I, I was already living in the United States and aware of America's involvement. I even responded to the call for service and underwent a physical examination as a draftee. Although I don't recall making any specific contributions, the war was a significant period during my early years in America.

The Mukden Incident, or Manchurian Incident, was a war that expanded Japan's influence into Manchuria, which was considered a lifeline for the nation. I listened to speeches by Japanese representatives who advocated this expansion, but as a Christian, I felt it was inexcusable to claim that land as Japan's own. This event marked a troubling time in Japan's foreign policy from my perspective.

I was born and raised in Japan, specifically in Ibaraki Prefecture, and spent my youth there until conscription. My family followed the Jodo sect of Buddhism, and life was generally peaceful during my childhood. I later returned to Japan briefly for my father's illness and marriage before settling permanently in the United States.

I attended middle school in Tokyo, where I lived as a young man during the Russo-Japanese War. It was in Tokyo that I received my education up to middle school level before eventually coming to the United States. The city was an important place in my formative years.

I came to the United States in 1907 aboard the Shinano Maru to study and pursue better opportunities. I initially lived in California, working in agriculture and eventually settling in Watsonville. Though I faced some discrimination, I adapted well and decided to stay permanently, marrying and raising my family here.

I traveled to America in 1907 on the ship called Shinano Maru, operated by Yusen Kaisha. The ship was mostly filled with young people like myself, many of whom were trying to enter the United States despite restrictive immigration laws. The journey was lively and memorable as we faced the challenges of immigration.

The Shinano Maru, the ship I took to America, was operated by Yusen Kaisha. This company was responsible for transporting many Japanese immigrants like me during that era. Their service was crucial in connecting Japan and the United States for those seeking new opportunities abroad.

My family belonged to the Jo-do sect of Buddhism while I was in Japan. This religious background shaped much of my early life before I later converted to Christianity after coming to the United States. The Jo-do teachings were part of my cultural heritage growing up in Ibaraki Prefecture.

I settled in Watsonville, California, where I began farming after arriving in the United States. It was here that I experienced the Great Depression's hardships as farm prices fell. Watsonville was also where I attended church services and was part of the local Japanese community during World War II.

I was involved with the Westview United Presbyterian Church in Watsonville, where I attended services regularly. It was at this church that I first came into contact with Reverend Arima, who played a role in my Christian baptism. The church community was important to me during my life in America.

My family's practical approach to life was influenced by the principles introduced by the late feudal Lord of Mito Domain. This legacy of practicality shaped how we faced life's challenges both in Japan and later in America. It was a guiding philosophy passed down through generations.

I did not receive much help from Japanese organizations such as the Japanese American Citizens League during my time in America. While these groups existed, they did not play a significant role in assisting me or my family. I mostly relied on my own efforts and faith to navigate life here.

Similar to the Japanese American Citizens League, the Kenjinkai did not provide much assistance to me during my years in the United States. Although these organizations were present, I did not find them particularly helpful in my personal or farming life. I focused more on my family and church community.

I came into contact with Reverend Arima around 1920 while I was farming in Watsonville. He had a small office and was active in the community, and through him and a man named Hiraura Kantoku, I was baptized into the Christian faith. Reverend Arima was important in my spiritual journey after coming to America.

Hiraura Kantoku was the man who baptized me into Christianity after I met Reverend Arima in Watsonville. His role was significant in my conversion from Buddhism to Christianity during the early 1920s. This marked a major change in my spiritual life while living in the United States.

During the Great Depression, I had just started farming in Watsonville and experienced the hardships as farm product prices dropped significantly. It was a difficult time with little restfulness, but I persevered through those economic challenges. The Depression tested my resolve as a farmer and immigrant.

During World War II, I lived in Watsonville and noticed that while there were not many harsh events against Japanese Americans, we were viewed as the enemy race and felt isolated. The war brought a sense of suspicion and separation from the wider community. Despite this, I continued farming and maintaining my faith.

When Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941, I was at church in Watsonville. The event shocked us all, and from my Christian perspective, I believed Japan had done something wrong with that surprise attack. It was a moment of deep concern and reflection for me and the community.

I learned about the relocation notices during World War II while I was farming in Watsonville. Although the transcript does not detail my time at the Watsonville Assembly Center, it was part of the forced relocation process that affected many Japanese Americans in the area. This was a difficult period for our community.

The transcript does not mention my direct experience at the Manzanar War Relocation Center, but it was one of the main camps where Japanese Americans were sent during the war. Like many in the community, I was aware of these centers and the hardships they represented. It was a painful chapter in our history.

After World War II, I moved to Dinuba, California, before eventually relocating to Ogden, Utah. This was part of my post-war resettlement as I sought to rebuild my life and continue farming. Dinuba was a stepping stone in my journey after the war.

Following my time in Dinuba, I moved to Ogden, Utah, as part of my post-war life. This move was part of the broader relocation many Japanese Americans experienced after the war. Ogden became one of the places where I sought stability and continued my life in America.

I was a member of the Presbyterian Church (USA) through my involvement with the Westview United Presbyterian Church in Watsonville. This church and its ministers, including Reverend Arima, were central to my Christian faith and community life in America. The church provided spiritual support during challenging times.

During World War II, I experienced the impact of Japanese American internment as I received relocation notices while farming in Watsonville. Although the transcript does not specify my internment camp, this event caused isolation and hardship for me and my community. It was a painful and defining experience of that era.
